subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — Ralph Nader and Third-Party Barriers
stimulus:
In the 2000 election, Ralph Nader won the Green Party nomination for president of the United States. While he trailed the Democratic and Republican candidates in the polls by a wide margin, Nader attracted large audiences in campaign appearances across the country.

In an interview from 2016, Nader discussed the challenges he faced in his campaign for president. "But there are only two that get on the presidential debate — Republican and Democrat — because they control the gate. . . . Had I got on the debates in my presidential run, I would — in one debate, I would have reached more people, by 50-fold, than I reached by filling all the major arenas. . . ."

"Never mind that you represent majoritarian positions, like full Medicare for all, like loosening up the electoral process so more people can get in and run and vote. It doesn't matter that you represent majoritarian positions that are taken off the table by the Republican and Democratic Party."

parts.prompt: Describe a structural barrier in the scenario that makes it less likely that a third-party candidate will be able to secure enough popular support to justify including the candidate in a debate.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Describes a structural barrier such as the winner-take-all electoral system, which discourages voters from supporting third-party candidates because they are less likely to win, so third-party candidates rarely have enough support to be included in debates. Accept other structural barriers such as ballot access laws, campaign finance rules, or lack of major party resources.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate structural barrier referenced in or suggested by the scenario. The response must describe the barrier, not just name it.

parts.prompt: In the context of the scenario, explain how a third-party candidate could still have an influence on public policy despite the barrier described in part A.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains how a third-party candidate could influence public policy despite structural barriers. Accept: third-party candidates like Nader can influence public policy by raising awareness of issues, leading major parties to adopt their platform; spoiler effect — third-party candidates can affect election outcomes even without winning; publishing platforms that attract media attention.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific mechanism for third-party influence on policy. Accept any plausible explanation grounded in the context of the scenario.

parts.prompt: Explain how including the third-party candidate in the scenario could have had a positive impact on participatory democracy.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains a positive impact on participatory democracy. Accept: participatory democracy encourages broad engagement, and including a third-party candidate in debates expands the appeal of the election and brings in more people and ideas; a third-party candidate like Nader expands the range of issues, motivating more people to participate in politics.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect third-party inclusion to the core principles of participatory democracy (broad participation, citizen engagement). Accept any accurate explanation.
